CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well organized and the main contributions (multitask pretraining of a central five-character window, a modular global transition-based segmentor, and empirical gains across CTB6 and multiple benchmarks) are stated early and clearly. Notation and methodological descriptions are reported with sufficient detail to include hyperparameters, and related work coverage is thorough, which supports reproducibility-minded readers. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, though from the summary I infer a few sections (e.g., the precise role of word-level LSTM removal and the ablation of each external resource) could benefit from clearer, more surgical exposition to match the paper’s strong empirical claims. | B(4): The paper is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions — modular global transition-based segmentor and multitask pretraining of a five-character window yielding strong CTB6 results — are stated early and clearly. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples (benchmarks across CTB6, SIGHAN, Weibo) are effective for demonstrating empirical gains. Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough, though a few minor presentation issues remain (e.g., explicit code release not mentioned and some dense sections could be streamlined for practitioners). Overall the paper communicates its approach and results clearly and practically.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well organized and the main contributions (multitask pretraining of a central five-character window, a modular global transition-based segmentor, and empirical gains across CTB6 and multiple benchmarks) are stated early and clearly. Notation and methodological descriptions are reported with sufficient detail to include hyperparameters, and related work coverage is thorough, which supports reproducibility-minded readers. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, though from the summary I infer a few sections (e.g., the precise role of word-level LSTM removal and the ablation of each external resource) could benefit from clearer, more surgical exposition to match the paper’s strong empirical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions — modular global transition-based segmentor and multitask pretraining of a five-character window yielding strong CTB6 results — are stated early and clearly. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples (benchmarks across CTB6, SIGHAN, Weibo) are effective for demonstrating empirical gains. Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough, though a few minor presentation issues remain (e.g., explicit code release not mentioned and some dense sections could be streamlined for practitioners). Overall the paper communicates its approach and results clearly and practically.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs comprehensive evaluations across CTB6, multiple SIGHAN datasets and Weibo and compares to a long list of strong baselines, and reports hyperparameters and structured inference details—supporting the main performance claims. However, from the summary I must deduct for insufficiently surgical analysis: the central claims about the five-character window and the contribution of each external pretraining source appear not to be isolated via controlled ablations (the multitask pretraining claim is reported as a whole ~14.5% error reduction but per-source contributions are not quantified). They do acknowledge at least one limitation (lexicon use) and provide many baselines, yet absence of mentioned code and limited failure-case analysis leave some reproducibility and interpretability gaps. | B(4): The paper provides strong empirical evaluation: multiple benchmarks (CTB6, SIGHAN bake-off sets, NLPCC Weibo) and comparisons to many competitive baselines, and reports a large reported error reduction from multitask pretraining. Appropriate ablations/sensitivity checks appear present (e.g., showing multitask pretraining gains and that a word-level LSTM is unnecessary), and training hyperparameters are reported. Remaining gaps are practical reproducibility (code not mentioned) and one acknowledged omission (no lexicon experiments), so the experimental evidence is solid but not completely exhaustive for full adoption.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs comprehensive evaluations across CTB6, multiple SIGHAN datasets and Weibo and compares to a long list of strong baselines, and reports hyperparameters and structured inference details—supporting the main performance claims. However, from the summary I must deduct for insufficiently surgical analysis: the central claims about the five-character window and the contribution of each external pretraining source appear not to be isolated via controlled ablations (the multitask pretraining claim is reported as a whole ~14.5% error reduction but per-source contributions are not quantified). They do acknowledge at least one limitation (lexicon use) and provide many baselines, yet absence of mentioned code and limited failure-case analysis leave some reproducibility and interpretability gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides strong empirical evaluation: multiple benchmarks (CTB6, SIGHAN bake-off sets, NLPCC Weibo) and comparisons to many competitive baselines, and reports a large reported error reduction from multitask pretraining. Appropriate ablations/sensitivity checks appear present (e.g., showing multitask pretraining gains and that a word-level LSTM is unnecessary), and training hyperparameters are reported. Remaining gaps are practical reproducibility (code not mentioned) and one acknowledged omission (no lexicon experiments), so the experimental evidence is solid but not completely exhaustive for full adoption.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear claims and provides extensive empirical evidence across multiple datasets and strong baselines, supporting the main technical contributions. However, there are modest methodological/reporting gaps—lack of isolated ablations for some pretraining components, limited theoretical justification for the five-character window, and missing statistical significance/code release—that prevent a top rating. Given the solid empirical gains but these reproducibility/analysis shortcomings, a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides extensive empirical results across CTB6, SIGHAN and Weibo, and compares to many strong baselines (including older statistical/hybrid systems). However, from the summary there is insufficient theoretical justification for why a central five-character window yields the reported inductive bias, and the crucial components of the multitask pretraining are not reported as isolated ablations with statistical testing (e.g. contribution of each external resource, and the redundancy of the word-level LSTM). Because these methodological gaps leave key claims under-supported—even if empirical gains look strong—I rate it as having some concerns about completeness of evidence rather than a fundamental flaw.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear well supported by extensive experiments across CTB6, multiple SIGHAN corpora, NLPCC Weibo and with multitask pretraining on Gigaword/People's Daily; reported gains (e.g. ~14.5% relative error reduction) and ablations (showing five-character window and character context importance) provide concrete empirical evidence. Mathematical content is minimal for this engineering paper and thus not a concern, and the methodology is largely sound—multiple competitive baselines are compared and hyperparameters are reported. Minor weaknesses: no mention of statistical significance testing or code release for full reproducibility, and the decision not to explore lexicons removes one practical comparison point. Overall the technical claims are credible and empirically supported, with only modest reproducibility/reporting gaps.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers a practical, non-trivial extension—multitask pretraining of a central five-character context module integrated into a modular global transition-based segmentor—that yields empirical gains but is not a radical theoretical advance. Given the clear empirical contribution, differentiation from prior work, and usefulness to practitioners, a slightly above-moderate originality score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a focused architectural and training innovation—multitask pretraining of a central five-character context window within a modular global transition-based neural segmentor—and shows empirical gains across standard CWS corpora. This is a non-trivial, practically useful extension of prior neural segmentors (and the claim that word-level LSTM context is unnecessary is a noteworthy empirical insight), but it largely combines existing ideas (transition-based segmentation, contextual window features, multitask pretraining on external corpora) rather than introducing a fundamentally new formulation or theory. From a theory-minded perspective the contribution is therefore moderate novelty: informative to practitioners but not a major conceptual advance.}

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and practical novelty in pretraining a shared five-character context window via multitask learning on diverse external resources and integrating it into a modular global transition-based neural segmentor — an approach that differs clearly from prior pure neural and statistical CWS models. The problem (Chinese word segmentation) is well-studied, but the technique of multitask pretraining of a central context module and the emphasis on modularity and pure neural global inference is a non-trivial extension over existing methods cited (e.g., Cai & Zhao 2016, Zhang et al. 2016). The authors clearly differentiate their contribution and demonstrate empirical gains, so while not a radical theoretical breakthrough, the method would inform practitioners and experts as a useful, adoptable advance.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides strong empirical gains on a central practical task and proposes a modular, easy-to-adopt multitask pretraining approach that is likely to influence practice. I rate impact as slightly above moderate because the clear empirical improvements and practical recipe make adoption likely, but the lack of ablations, mechanistic explanation, and code release mean it falls short of being transformative for the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical task (neural Chinese word segmentation) and reports strong empirical gains — multitask pretraining of a central five-character window leads to state-of-the-art CTB6 results and large reported error reductions across several benchmarks. However, from a theory-minded methodological standpoint its impact is limited because the work leans heavily on external-resource pretraining without documented ablations quantifying each resource’s contribution, and key architectural claims (importance of the five-character window, redundancy of the word-level LSTM) demand more controlled experiments and mechanistic explanation to change how researchers think about model design. Given solid empirical performance and broad baselines but lacking the sort of principled analysis that would reshape practice, I assess the contribution as moderate and likely to be useful but not field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a central practical problem (neural Chinese word segmentation) and provides strong empirical evidence: a modular, multitask-pretrained five-character context yields state-of-the-art results on CTB6 and reports a substantial ~14.5% relative error reduction using multiple external resources. The method is practically appealing and easily adoptable by practitioners (modular design, clear training recipe, leverages heterogeneous corpora), so it is likely to be widely used and to shift practice toward targeted multitask pretraining. It stops short of being transformative because there is no mention of released code and the ideas are an effective engineering advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, though it does open useful directions for resource-aware pretraining.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is well within the venue scope, addresses an important task, and delivers strong empirical results with a solid modular architecture and multitask pretraining. Reviewer A’s concerns about missing mechanistic analysis, controlled ablations, and unclear reproducibility temper enthusiasm, so I rate it a 4 to reflect a strong fit but with methodological gaps that should be resolved for top-tier acceptance.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: neural Chinese word segmentation with careful evaluation on CTB6, SIGHAN datasets and multiple baselines (including older statistical/hybrid work) addresses the right audience. The modular transition-based model and multitask pretraining on multiple external corpora adopt methods and metrics expected at the venue. However, from the summary I worry about missing mechanistic analysis and controlled ablations (e.g. per-resource contribution, explicit tests of the claimed redundancy of the word-level LSTM), and code/reproducibility is not mentioned — issues that reduce its methodological sharpness for a top-tier acceptance. Overall it is a strong fit but would benefit from deeper theoretical and ablation studies to fully satisfy the standards I expect.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles an important core NLP task (Chinese word segmentation) with a practical, modular neural architecture and demonstrates substantial empirical gains (SOTA on CTB6 and large relative error reductions) across multiple standard benchmarks. The use of multitask pretraining with diverse external resources, beam-search structured learning, and thorough comparisons/ablation-style claims align well with the expectations of the community and target audience. Reporting of hyperparameters and thorough related work coverage further support reproducibility and adoption, making the work highly relevant to conference attendees and practitioners.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares to a broad set of recent strong baselines on standard datasets (CTB6, SIGHAN, Weibo) and uses standard P/R/F metrics, making the evaluation broadly fair and reproducible. While Reviewer A notes some minor missing isolation (e.g., per-resource pretraining ablations) that would increase transparency, Reviewer B’s assessment that the comparisons are comprehensive, well-positioned, and limitations are acknowledged leads me to judge the overall comparison as meaningful and sufficient, so I award the top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of recent neural and classical baselines (e.g., Zhang et al. 2016; Cai and Zhao 2016; Zhang and Clark 2007; Pei et al. 2014) on standard benchmarks (CTB6, SIGHAN, Weibo) using standard P/R/F1 metrics, so coverage is strong. However, from the summary it is unclear whether all comparisons fully isolate contributions (e.g., per-resource pretraining ablations) or whether any competing methods occasionally match or outperform the proposal are explicitly acknowledged, which prevents me from awarding the top score for comprehensive, fully transparent comparisons.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on the standard CTB6 and multiple SIGHAN/NLPCC datasets and compares to a broad set of recent strong baselines (e.g. Cai & Zhao 2016; Zhang et al. 2016; Pei et al. 2014), reporting P/R/F and hyperparameters. Comparisons appear fair and reproducible (same data and metrics), and the authors explicitly position their results as outperforming prior pure-neural and many statistical models while acknowledging limits (e.g. they did not use lexicons and explain why). The manuscript also clearly analyzes how its multitask pretraining and modular five-character context differ from prior work and discusses related approaches thoroughly, so I judge the experimental comparisons comprehensive and appropriate.

main_claims: Pretraining a central five-character context window with rich external resources significantly improves neural Chinese word segmentation performance.
A modular, pure neural segmentation model with multitask pretraining achieves state-of-the-art results on CTB6, outperforming prior pure neural and many statistical models.
Multitask pretraining using multiple external sources yields substantial relative error reduction (reported as ~14.5%).
Word-level LSTM context is unnecessary for their model; character contexts contribute relatively more and the model is conceptually simpler and more modular.
_summary.methodology: A modular global transition-based neural segmentor that shares and pretrains a five-character context window via multitask learning on multiple external data sources, combined with beam-search structured learning and inference.
datasets: People's Daily corpus (for heterogenous segmentation and POS pretraining)
limitations: They did not investigate use of lexicons because lexicons may cover different OOVs in training and test data, complicating fair comparisons.

reviewer_a.name: Theory-minded Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a track record of work on model interpretability and theoretical analysis of learning algorithms.
traits: Penalises empirical papers that lack a clear theoretical or algorithmic justification of why the proposed architecture should improve over alternatives (will deduct points if no analysis of inductive bias or guarantees is provided).

Requires systematic ablation: will downgrade if critical claims (e.g., central five-character window importance, redundancy of word-level LSTM) are not isolated via controlled experiments and statistical significance tests.
Penalises reliance on large external resources unless the incremental benefit from each resource is quantified (will deduct points if contributions from each pretraining source are not ablated).
Demands comparisons against strong non-neural and hybrid baselines (will penalise papers with fewer than 3 established baselines including at least one statistical/hybrid model).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Skeptical, focuses on mechanistic understanding and reproducibility: gives credit for clear theoretical motivation and surgically controlled experiments, deducts for empirical-only gains without analysis.

reviewer_b.name: Pragmatic Empiricalist
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in NLP with extensive experience building and deploying state-of-the-art sequence models and pretraining pipelines for applied tasks.
traits: Rewards papers that demonstrate clear empirical gains on standard benchmarks (will award points for reproducible SOTA results, especially if >0.5% absolute or >10% relative error reduction is reported).
Positively biases toward clever use of external data and multitask pretraining (will give extra credit when multiple external resources are leveraged and the method is modular and practical).
Favors engineering clarity and reproducibility: expects modular design, clear training recipes, and (preferably) code/data release; will rate higher if the method appears easy to adopt and extend.
Does not heavily penalise lack of formal theory as long as empirical evaluation is thorough and ablations show the method's robustness across datasets/conditions.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Practical and results-driven: emphasizes solid empirical evidence, clear gains, and reproducible, modular designs that can be adopted by practitioners.
